title: LDAP Data Structure
text: LDAP Data Structure LDAP data is stored in a record-like structure, called an entry. No table or field definition information is stored in the LDAP environment. Instead, each entry contains its own field definitions. All LDAP entries contain at least one object class field. Object classes are defined on the LDAP server as a set of mandatory and optional fields. Field Types Object class definitions also specify the data type of each field. LDAP recognizes the following field types: Single-value string fields (fixed length) Multiple-value string fields (values are separated by a newline character) Binary data fields Tree Structure LDAP organizes data into a hierarchical tree structure where each node in the tree represents an entry in a database. Entries correspond to a real-world object, that is, a person, department, organization, or country. They are collections of attribute-value pairs and are identified unambiguously by a distinguished name. A distinguished name is a combination of the entry name and the names of its parent entries, and is unique for all entries in a database. Typical attributes include the following: o —organization ou —organizational unit c —country givenname —given name cn —common name email —email address telephonenumber —telephone number Related Links LDAP The Lghtweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) is a protocol for accessing online directory services. It offers common access to a centralized facility for storing and maintaining user access privilege, email, and sign-on information which can be shared among Web servers, mail servers, news servers and Uniface applications. Data Storage and Retrieval on LDAP LDAP differs substantially in structure from traditional relational databases. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
